Hazard: Field layout congestion or unsafe proximity between assets, pipelines, umbilicals, and future access routes
Causes: Immature layout; weak spacing rules; limited intervention input; routing optimized for CAPEX only
Consequences: Clashes; unsafe installation and intervention; damage exposure; redesign later in lifecycle
ExistingSafeguards: Concept reviews; preliminary spacing standards; route corridor screening
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Define minimum spacing and crossing rules; reserve intervention corridors; compare options with visual HAZID before concept freeze
AppliesTo: Subsea architecture, manifolds, flowlines, umbilicals, topsides tie-ins

Hazard: Unrecognized geohazard or seabed instability affecting foundations and routing
Causes: Incomplete survey coverage; shallow gas; boulders; slope instability; scour; mobile seabed
Consequences: Foundation instability; free spans; installation delay; integrity failure in service
ExistingSafeguards: Desktop studies; preliminary geophysical and geotechnical screening
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Flag geohazard zones in model; require survey confidence rating; hold route decisions where subsurface data is weak
AppliesTo: Templates, manifolds, PLETs, PLEMs, pipelines, cables

Hazard: Concept assumes marine execution that is unsafe or impractical
Causes: No early vessel strategy; underestimated crane reach or deck load; poor weather assumptions; limited marine input
Consequences: Offshore campaign delay; collision or dropped object risk; redesign of structures and lifting points
ExistingSafeguards: High-level installation philosophy; marine consultation at concept stage
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Add vessel and installation specialist review; capture lift envelopes, access corridors, and vessel assumptions in model metadata
AppliesTo: Heavy lifts, subsea installation spreads, offshore hookup

Hazard: Major hydrocarbon release scenario not designed out at concept stage
Causes: Weak isolation philosophy; poor segregation; vulnerable tie-in arrangement; immature leak detection concept
Consequences: Loss of containment; fire or explosion; environmental release; major redesign later
ExistingSafeguards: Conceptual shutdown and isolation philosophy; hazard review workshops
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Compare concepts on escalation potential, segmentation, shutdown, and depressurization; make major accident scenarios mandatory review prompts
AppliesTo: Processing systems, export systems, tie-ins, brownfield connections

Hazard: Unsafe access or poor maintainability designed into equipment and field arrangement
Causes: Operability input missing; focus on normal operation only; access envelope not considered
Consequences: Unsafe interventions; deferred maintenance; high OPEX; procedural workarounds
ExistingSafeguards: Maintainability philosophy; operations representative input
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Record access constraints and retrieval assumptions in design; add maintainability prompts to all concept HAZIDs
AppliesTo: Subsea retrievable equipment, valves, topside skids, access routes

Hazard: Future tie-in or expansion constraints embedded in initial concept
Causes: No future capacity allowance; crowded hubs; routing locked early; no spare control or hydraulic capacity
Consequences: Unsafe brownfield tie-ins later; higher future CAPEX; operational disruption
ExistingSafeguards: Concept option review; high-level growth assumptions
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Reserve future tie-in corridors and capacity; tag expansion assumptions and unresolved constraints in model
AppliesTo: Tie-ins, spare slots, controls, utility capacity

Hazard: Emergency response assumptions do not fit field concept or marine spread
Causes: Weak scenario definition; poor standby vessel assumptions; access or rescue routes not considered
Consequences: Delayed rescue; escalation during incident; weak evacuation strategy
ExistingSafeguards: Emergency response philosophy; conceptual ERP assumptions
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Include emergency access, rescue, and standby assumptions in concept review; recheck for remote or harsh environments
AppliesTo: Remote subsea fields, offshore facilities, marine campaigns

Hazard: Environmental discharge or spill pathways not recognized early
Causes: Poor containment concept; chemical handling assumptions; weak drainage and storage philosophy
Consequences: Pollution event; permit issues; cleanup cost; reputational damage
ExistingSafeguards: Environmental design basis; early environmental studies
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Add environmental consequence class to early hazards; identify sensitive receptors and spill pathways in design basis
AppliesTo: Produced fluids, chemicals, storage, offloading, drilling support

Hazard: Interface mismatch between subsea, topsides, controls, and marine packages
Causes: Split package ownership; late changes; mismatched tag sets; unclear interface boundaries
Consequences: Integration failures; commissioning delay; wrong hookups; unsafe operation
ExistingSafeguards: Interface registers; coordination meetings; design reviews
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Use FieldTwin as shared interface review context; assign interface owners; create mandatory prompts for mechanical, control, hydraulic, and electrical interfaces
AppliesTo: Package interfaces across EPC and operator scopes

Hazard: Isolation and depressurization philosophy is inadequate for maintenance or upset conditions
Causes: Valve placement gaps; trapped volumes; weak vent or drain concept; poor shutdown logic
Consequences: Release during maintenance; escalation during upset; unsafe restart
ExistingSafeguards: P&IDs; shutdown philosophy; discipline reviews
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Review intrusive maintenance scenarios early; tag isolation points and trapped inventory risks against systems and line segments
AppliesTo: Process systems, subsea tie-ins, utility systems

Hazard: Control system failure modes or alarm philosophy are not adequately understood
Causes: Incomplete cause and effect; poor redundancy; weak communications assumptions; package integration issues
Consequences: Missed leak or upset; spurious trips; unstable operations; startup problems
ExistingSafeguards: Control narratives; F&G concept; shutdown philosophy
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Add controls-specific HAZID prompts; track unresolved alarm, trip, and fail-safe assumptions as actions before IFC
AppliesTo: SCADA, subsea controls, ESD, F&G, communications

Hazard: Chemical selection or material compatibility issue creates safety or integrity risk
Causes: Incorrect chemical assumptions; incompatible elastomers or metallurgy; wrong injection point; handling gaps
Consequences: Exposure to personnel; corrosion; plugging; release; environmental impact
ExistingSafeguards: Chemical management process; materials reviews; SDS controls
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Add chemical and compatibility prompts by system; link storage, dosing, and handling assumptions to affected assets
AppliesTo: Flow assurance systems, inhibition systems, utility chemicals

Hazard: SIMOPS conflict between drilling, construction, lifting, energization, or marine operations
Causes: Campaign overlap; shared deck or sea space; weak permit and schedule integration
Consequences: Collision; dropped objects; work stoppage; personnel exposure; emergency complexity
ExistingSafeguards: SIMOPS plan; PTW; marine coordination meetings
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Create scenario-based SIMOPS hazards and exclusion zones; force review of concurrent activities during FEED and campaign planning
AppliesTo: Brownfield projects, offshore hook-up, drilling overlap

Hazard: Environmental or metocean loading assumptions are inadequate
Causes: Outdated data; weak fatigue basis; poor extreme event assumptions; incomplete seasonal planning
Consequences: Overstress; installation abort; accelerated damage; downtime
ExistingSafeguards: Design basis documents; metocean studies
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Tag metocean basis to affected assets and routes; flag low-confidence assumptions; trigger re-review when basis changes
AppliesTo: Moorings, flexible risers, structures, campaigns

Hazard: Temporary construction, testing, or commissioning systems introduce unmanaged risk
Causes: Temporary hoses, jumpers, bypasses, generators, or controls not fully reviewed
Consequences: Unexpected release, trip, overload, or personnel exposure
ExistingSafeguards: Temporary equipment procedures; review gates
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Treat temporary systems as first-class review objects; require explicit hazard review and ownership before use
AppliesTo: Commissioning packages, temporary power, bypasses, hoses

Hazard: Procedures, alarms, controls, or access arrangements are vulnerable to human error
Causes: Poor labeling; dense interfaces; weak visibility; ergonomic issues; excessive manual steps
Consequences: Wrong operation; delayed response; maintenance errors; nuisance trips
ExistingSafeguards: Procedures; HMI reviews; labeling standards
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Include human factors prompts in FEED; require critical task walkthroughs and positive identification points
AppliesTo: Control rooms, local panels, valve lineups, intervention tasks

Hazard: Late design change introduces new hazards without structured reassessment
Causes: Vendor deviation; procurement substitution; route change; schedule pressure; poor MOC discipline
Consequences: Hidden incompatibility; unsafe installation or operation; offshore rework
ExistingSafeguards: Document revision control; change approval workflow
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Trigger HAZID re-review for defined change classes; visually highlight changed objects and unresolved hazards before issue status advances
AppliesTo: All systems and packages

Hazard: Vendor package assumptions conflict with project design intent
Causes: Incomplete requisition; black-box design; missing limits or interlocks; late clarifications
Consequences: Integration failure; unsafe operating envelope; commissioning delay
ExistingSafeguards: Vendor document review; FAT; interface meetings
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Capture vendor assumptions as hazards; link package constraints and design limits to assets and systems in model
AppliesTo: Compressors, pumps, umbilicals, control systems, skids

Hazard: Fabrication, loadout, transport, or lifting damages equipment or creates major lifting risk
Causes: Weak lift point design; sea fastening issues; weather exposure; rushed execution; poor transport analysis
Consequences: Dropped object; latent equipment damage; schedule slip; offshore failure
ExistingSafeguards: Lift studies; transport procedures; inspections; preservation plans
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Review logistics hazards for large structures; store handling limits and protected zones as metadata; verify transport and installation assumptions remain aligned
AppliesTo: Modules, subsea structures, reels, umbilicals, valves

Hazard: Detailed design misses local overstress, fatigue hotspot, or unsupported span risk
Causes: Load cases omitted; interface loads uncertain; installation loads underestimated; span growth not managed
Consequences: Premature failure; integrity intervention; shutdown
ExistingSafeguards: Structural analysis; fatigue checks; design reviews
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Track analysis assumptions and weak points as linked findings; require closure evidence before fabrication release
AppliesTo: Pipelines, jumpers, manifolds, supports, clamps

Hazard: Critical equipment or materials lack manufacturing or inspection traceability
Causes: Poor data handover; missing certs; inconsistent serial tracking; supplier quality escape
Consequences: Latent failure; inability to verify fitness for service; rework
ExistingSafeguards: ITPs; QA/QC process; certification requirements
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Link traceability records and inspection status to critical assets; flag incomplete records before shipment or installation
AppliesTo: Pressure-containing equipment, safety-critical elements

Hazard: Stored equipment degrades before installation or startup
Causes: Poor preservation; wrong storage conditions; moisture ingress; expired consumables or seals
Consequences: Latent failure at startup; leak; control issue; replacement cost
ExistingSafeguards: Preservation plans; warehouse controls; receiving inspections
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Treat storage condition and preservation expiry as tracked risks for long-lead or critical equipment
AppliesTo: Instruments, valves, subsea electronics, elastomers

Hazard: Detailed design is difficult or unsafe to fabricate, assemble, or install
Causes: Design tolerance stack-up; inaccessible welds; weak rigging arrangement; poor sequence planning
Consequences: Unsafe workarounds; quality issues; delay; added offshore scope
ExistingSafeguards: Constructability reviews; fabrication input; 3D model checks
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Run formal constructability review against the design model; capture fabrication and installation sequence hazards as actions
AppliesTo: Modules, pipe racks, subsea structures, brownfield modifications

Hazard: Control or communications architecture introduces resilience or security weakness
Causes: Unclear network boundaries; shared access; weak failover; unmanaged remote connectivity
Consequences: Loss of monitoring or control; unstable operation; delayed incident response
ExistingSafeguards: Network standards; access control; redundancy philosophy
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Add cyber-resilience and comms failure prompts for critical control interfaces; record fallback operating mode assumptions
AppliesTo: SCADA, remote ops, subsea comms, offshore control networks

Hazard: Construction or support vessel collides with offshore structure or subsea infrastructure
Causes: DP failure; watchkeeping failure; congested worksite; bad weather; poor marine coordination
Consequences: Structural damage; hydrocarbon release; injury; campaign shutdown
ExistingSafeguards: Marine procedures; exclusion zones; DP trials; bridge watch protocols
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Display vessel approach and standby zones in model; require collision-risk review for each campaign spread and nearby assets
AppliesTo: Platforms, FPSOs, jackets, subsea fields, support vessels

Hazard: Load or equipment is dropped during lifting, overboarding, or subsea deployment
Causes: Rigging failure; crane overload; poor weather; weak lift planning; human error
Consequences: Fatality; equipment damage; line or umbilical damage; delay
ExistingSafeguards: Lift plans; certified rigging; toolbox talks; weather criteria; exclusion zones
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Create dropped-object hazards by campaign and lift; identify vulnerable lines and equipment under lift paths
AppliesTo: Heavy lifts, deployment frames, subsea modules, deck lifts

Hazard: Line, cable, control, or jumper is connected, lined up, or energized incorrectly
Causes: Tag confusion; poor as-built visibility; interface mismatch; rushed handover
Consequences: Equipment damage; loss of control; startup failure; unsafe pressurization
ExistingSafeguards: Checklists; labeling; verification steps; PTW
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Require positive identification and model-linked verification before hookup or energization; make redline and as-built validation mandatory
AppliesTo: Electrical tie-ins, jumpers, valves, controls, umbilicals

Hazard: Commissioning proceeds on incomplete, impaired, or unsafe system
Causes: Schedule pressure; weak punch-list discipline; open overrides; unresolved hazards
Consequences: Trip, release, equipment damage, unsafe manual intervention
ExistingSafeguards: Commissioning procedures; readiness reviews; punch lists
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Show critical open HAZID actions by system before startup; require explicit sign-off for overrides, bypasses, and temporary arrangements
AppliesTo: System turnover, energization, startup

Hazard: Subsea structure, jumper, or tool lands out of tolerance or on wrong target
Causes: Survey mismatch; poor visibility; vessel motion; inadequate guide features; weak communication
Consequences: Damage to connectors or structures; leak path; retrieval campaign; delay
ExistingSafeguards: Survey controls; landing procedures; ROV support; installation aids
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Track landing tolerances, no-contact zones, and critical mating surfaces as visible review objects in campaign planning
AppliesTo: Trees, manifolds, jumpers, caps, flying leads

Hazard: Pressure testing creates unsafe stored-energy or release scenario
Causes: Incorrect test boundary; trapped air; wrong test medium; poor exclusion control; weak reinstatement
Consequences: Release, injury, equipment damage, false acceptance, startup problems
ExistingSafeguards: Test packs; procedures; boundary checks; barriers and exclusion zones
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Treat each test boundary as a review object; force review of stored energy, venting, drains, and reinstatement steps
AppliesTo: Hydrotest, leak test, pneumatic test, FAT/SAT

Hazard: Weather window or environmental exposure assumptions fail during offshore campaign
Causes: Rapid weather change; underestimated sea state; poor contingency; vessel limits exceeded
Consequences: Unsafe lifting or transfer; aborted operation; equipment damage; personnel exposure
ExistingSafeguards: Weather criteria; forecast process; go/no-go authority
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Capture campaign-specific weather limits and contingency triggers in planning; tie them to lift and deployment tasks
AppliesTo: Marine operations, transfers, lifts, ROV spread

Hazard: PTW or work control breakdown during high-activity offshore execution
Causes: Too many concurrent permits; weak handover; poor area authority; copy-paste risk assessments
Consequences: Conflicting work; line of fire exposure; isolation conflict; emergency confusion
ExistingSafeguards: PTW system; toolbox talks; SIMOPS meetings; supervision
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Add explicit SIMOPS and PTW conflict review for critical campaign periods; keep live list of high-risk concurrent tasks
AppliesTo: Hook-up, brownfield works, shutdown windows

Hazard: Hydrocarbon or hazardous fluid release during startup or ramp-up
Causes: Immature operating windows; wrong line-up; seal failure; instrumentation issues; overpressure
Consequences: Fire or explosion; toxic exposure; environmental impact; production loss
ExistingSafeguards: Startup procedures; leak detection; shutdown systems; supervision
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Use startup-specific hazard prompts and barrier checks; focus on temporary impairments and unresolved punch items before ramp-up
AppliesTo: Process systems, export, injection, utilities

Hazard: Existing safeguards are bypassed, unavailable, or degraded without sufficient control
Causes: Overrides; delayed maintenance; alarm flood; poor impairment process; test backlog
Consequences: Small upset escalates; weakened emergency response; latent major accident risk
ExistingSafeguards: Maintenance system; impairment register; audit and verification processes
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Add safeguard effectiveness and impairment status fields to each finding; trigger review when critical barriers are degraded
AppliesTo: Safety-critical elements, alarms, shutdowns, relief, F&G

Hazard: Operations team starts up with incomplete or unrealistic procedures
Causes: Late handover; weak procedure validation; vendor dependence; poor scenario testing
Consequences: Wrong line-up; unstable operation; manual workarounds; near misses
ExistingSafeguards: Operating procedures; startup training; shift supervision
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Link hazards to critical tasks and procedures; require field validation and abnormal scenario walkthroughs before handover closeout
AppliesTo: Control room, local operations, startup and shutdown

Hazard: Alarm overload or nuisance trips reduce operator response quality
Causes: Poor rationalization; temporary alarms left active; bad thresholds; commissioning leftovers
Consequences: Missed critical condition; unstable plant; operator error; production disruption
ExistingSafeguards: Alarm management process; control narratives; historian and tuning
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Track alarm-related hazards as startup actions; focus on bad actor alarms and high-consequence trip paths
AppliesTo: Control systems, F&G, process alarms

Hazard: Startup or transient operation causes hydrate, wax, slugging, or thermal upset
Causes: Procedure mismatch; wrong chemical dose; temperature or pressure transient; blocked line
Consequences: Loss of flow; overpressure; intervention campaign; equipment damage
ExistingSafeguards: Flow assurance design basis; chemical injection; operating envelopes
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Link flow assurance assumptions to systems and startup procedures; treat envelope excursions as hazard review triggers
AppliesTo: Subsea production, injection, export systems

Hazard: High manual workload during startup exposes personnel to line-of-fire, chemicals, noise, or hot work conflicts
Causes: Frequent local interventions; temporary works; incomplete remote control; weak coordination
Consequences: Injury; exposure; procedural deviation; delayed startup
ExistingSafeguards: PPE; PTW; toolbox talks; supervision; area controls
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Review manual intervention hotspots during startup; reduce need for repeated local actions or improve controls and access
AppliesTo: Platforms, modules, local valve or panel operations

Hazard: As-built, tag, or operating data quality is too poor for safe routine operation
Causes: Incomplete redlines; inconsistent tag mapping; poor document turnover; metadata gaps
Consequences: Wrong maintenance action; wrong isolation; wrong troubleshooting; unsafe decisions
ExistingSafeguards: Turnover process; document control; walkdowns
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Make as-built and tag validation a tracked startup hazard; prevent closeout until critical metadata gaps are resolved
AppliesTo: CMMS, control systems, digital twin, drawings, procedures

Hazard: Emergency response arrangements are not validated for actual operating condition
Causes: ERP based on design assumptions only; weak drills; changed marine spread or occupancy
Consequences: Slow response; ineffective muster or rescue; escalation
ExistingSafeguards: ERP; drills; standby vessel; communications plan
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Re-run emergency scenario review at startup and after key scope changes; tie scenarios to real layout and occupancy
AppliesTo: Operating facilities, remote fields, campaign transitions

Hazard: Corrosion, erosion, fatigue, or mechanical degradation leads to failure in service
Causes: Aging equipment; upset conditions; poor inhibitor performance; vibration; external damage
Consequences: Leak; production loss; major repair; environmental incident
ExistingSafeguards: Integrity management; inspection and monitoring; corrosion control
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Use the module for recurring hazard review on degraded barriers and overdue integrity actions; link findings to affected assets and circuits
AppliesTo: Piping, pipelines, valves, structures, pressure equipment

Hazard: Intrusive work exposes personnel to trapped pressure, hazardous energy, or residual inventory
Causes: Poor line-up; hidden sources; non-return valves; inadequate venting or draining; weak reinstatement
Consequences: Release; injury or fatality; ignition; extended shutdown
ExistingSafeguards: Isolation procedures; PTW; LOTO; gas testing; supervision
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Prompt for trapped pressure, residual fluids, hidden sources, and reinstatement steps; link isolation hazards to specific equipment and lines
AppliesTo: Maintenance, sampling, opening equipment, brownfield works

Hazard: Intervention activity damages subsea equipment or connectors
Causes: Poor visibility; inaccurate position data; weak tooling interface; vessel motion; human error
Consequences: Damage to valves, jumpers, connectors, or umbilicals; release risk; extra campaign cost
ExistingSafeguards: IMR procedures; qualified tooling; survey data; training
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Model intervention envelopes and no-contact zones; preserve prior design-stage findings as prompts for IMR campaigns
AppliesTo: ROV tasks, valve ops, connector work, inspection campaigns

Hazard: Anchor, trawl, dropped object, or third-party activity damages subsea infrastructure
Causes: Insufficient protection; poor charting; marine encroachment; fishing activity
Consequences: Leak; outage; repair campaign; environmental release
ExistingSafeguards: Burial and protection; exclusion zones; notifications; monitoring
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Flag high-exposure corridors and protection status in model; maintain area-based external aggression hazards and inspections
AppliesTo: Pipelines, umbilicals, cables, risers

Hazard: Tie-in or modification to live system creates unmanaged interaction with existing operations
Causes: Weak isolation; incomplete as-built understanding; SIMOPS; unknown condition of legacy equipment
Consequences: Release; startup delay; hidden damage; prolonged outage
ExistingSafeguards: Shutdown planning; isolation studies; brownfield procedures; SIMOPS controls
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Run dedicated brownfield tie-in HAZID; show changed objects and nearby live systems visually; require readiness review before cut-in
AppliesTo: Live modifications, hot taps, tie-ins, shutdown scopes

Hazard: Routine marine logistics or personnel transfer creates collision or transfer risk
Causes: Poor approach planning; weather exposure; inadequate communication; deck congestion
Consequences: Injury; dropped loads; vessel contact; evacuation complications
ExistingSafeguards: Transfer procedures; marine standards; weather criteria; deck management
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Track high-risk marine interfaces as recurring operational hazards; include attendant vessel and transfer scenarios in reviews
AppliesTo: Crew change, resupply, standby, support vessels

Hazard: Operational spill, vent, or discharge occurs during routine work or upset
Causes: Hose failure; leak; overfill; poor containment; maintenance error; chemical mishandling
Consequences: Pollution event; regulatory action; cleanup and downtime
ExistingSafeguards: Environmental procedures; bunding; spill kits; leak detection; response plans
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Add environmental consequence and spill pathway fields to hazards; track prevention and response readiness actions by area
AppliesTo: Chemicals, fuel, produced water, drains, transfer systems

Hazard: Competence, supervision, or organizational change weakens hazard control over time
Causes: Turnover; contractor changes; unclear accountability; poor learning capture; workload pressure
Consequences: Procedure drift; missed warning signs; poor response to abnormal situations; repeated incidents
ExistingSafeguards: Training; competence system; audits; shift handover; lessons learned
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Reassess hazards after major staffing, contractor, or organizational changes; keep ownership and action accountability explicit
AppliesTo: Operations, maintenance, campaigns, control room, offshore supervision

Hazard: Pipeline walking and thermal expansion overstresses tie-ins
Causes: Thermal cycling during production shutdowns; inadequate seabed friction; poor expansion loop design
Consequences: Jumper rupture; pipeline buckle; loss of containment; connector fatigue
ExistingSafeguards: Expansion spools; rock dumping; engineered anchors
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Conduct thermal walking analysis; specify and install engineered buckle triggers or sleepers; monitor expansion behavior post-startup
AppliesTo: Flowlines, jumpers, PLETs

Hazard: Severe terrain-induced slugging overloads topside facilities
Causes: Low flow rates during late life; undulating pipeline profile; inadequate liquid management
Consequences: Topside separator overflow; process trip; flaring event; mechanical fatigue on risers
ExistingSafeguards: Slug catchers; dynamic process simulation; active choke controls
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Size topside receiving facilities for maximum expected slug volume; implement automated active choke control sequences
AppliesTo: Risers, flowlines, topside separators

Hazard: Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV) causes rapid fatigue failure of risers or pipeline spans
Causes: Ocean currents passing over unsupported sections; insufficient structural damping
Consequences: Premature riser failure; catastrophic loss of containment; subsea span collapse
ExistingSafeguards: VIV strakes; fairings; span correction; fatigue analysis
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Apply VIV suppression devices to exposed risers; ensure max allowable free spans are documented and corrected during lay
AppliesTo: Risers, free-spanning pipelines, tubular structures

Hazard: Umbilical crushing or over-bending during deployment
Causes: Excessive tension on lay chute; incorrect bend radius controls; vessel heave
Consequences: Damage to internal steel tubes; loss of electrical continuity; fluid leak; complete functional failure
ExistingSafeguards: Tension limiters; lay analysis; bend restrictors; deck handling procedures
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Enforce strict lay tension monitoring; utilize bend stiffeners at critical transition points; perform continuous continuity checks during lay
AppliesTo: Umbilicals, power cables, flying leads

Hazard: Mooring line failure during vessel hookup or early operations
Causes: Chain fatigue; incorrect tensioning during installation; extreme weather conditions prior to full securement
Consequences: Vessel drift; riser rupture; collision with adjacent facilities
ExistingSafeguards: Tension load cells; chain inspections; weather window forecasting
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Monitor line tensions continuously during hookup; establish strict go/no-go weather limits for heading adjustments
AppliesTo: FPSOs, semi-submersibles, offloading buoys

Hazard: Hydraulic fluid contamination leads to Subsea Control Module (SCM) failure
Causes: Poor cleanliness during make-up; water ingress through degraded seals; particulate generation
Consequences: Directional control valve sticking; inability to operate subsea tree; loss of well control
ExistingSafeguards: Fluid cleanliness standards (NAS 1638/ASNT); subsea filters; fluid sampling
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Perform routine topside hydraulic fluid sampling and flushing; design SCMs with internal redundant filtration
AppliesTo: SCMs, umbilicals, subsea distribution units

Hazard: Gas blow-by from High Pressure (HP) to Low Pressure (LP) separator
Causes: Failure of liquid level control valve in open position; open manual bypass valves
Consequences: LP system overpressure; rupture of downstream vessels; lifting of multiple relief valves
ExistingSafeguards: Independent high-level trips; bursting discs; PSVs on LP system
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Maintain rigorous testing schedule for safety-critical level transmitters; size LP relief systems for full blow-by scenarios
AppliesTo: Separators, process vessels, relief systems

Hazard: Insufficient topside power allocation for future subsea boosting/compression
Causes: Weak initial load list estimation; failure to account for late-life reservoir pressure drop
Consequences: Deferred production; requirement for expensive brownfield power module integration
ExistingSafeguards: Phased power generation philosophy; electrical load flow studies
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Allocate physical deck space and weight capacity for future power modules; pre-invest in switchgear expansion slots
AppliesTo: Topside generators, electrical switchrooms, subsea power hubs

Hazard: Internal pipeline corrosion driven by sour gas (H2S/CO2) or high water cut
Causes: Material selection mismatch; continuous failure of chemical inhibitor injection
Consequences: Rapid wall thickness loss; localized pitting; pipeline rupture
ExistingSafeguards: Corrosion Resistant Alloy (CRA) cladding; chemical injection skids; corrosion coupons
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Specify CRA materials for high-risk segments; implement reliable subsea chemical injection and regular intelligent pigging
AppliesTo: Flowlines, manifolds, production headers

Hazard: Seabed scour compromises subsea foundation stability
Causes: Strong bottom currents mobilizing loose seabed soils around skirts or mudmats
Consequences: Foundation settlement; structural tilt; excessive stress transferred to connected jumpers
ExistingSafeguards: Scour protection mats; rock dumping; geotechnical modeling
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Install targeted scour protection immediately post-installation; perform periodic ROV visual surveys of foundation perimeters
AppliesTo: Manifolds, PLETs, templates, jacket legs

Hazard: Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC) from stagnant hydrotest water
Causes: Delayed dewatering schedule; inadequate application of biocide or oxygen scavengers
Consequences: Rapid internal pitting; premature pipeline failure before production begins
ExistingSafeguards: Treated test water; strict dewatering/drying schedules; preservation fluids
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Execute dewatering and nitrogen purging immediately following hydrotest acceptance; inject properly dosed biocide if water must remain
AppliesTo: Pipelines, flowlines, topside piping

Hazard: Complete loss of instrument air supply to offshore facility
Causes: Air compressor trip; catastrophic rupture of main air distribution header
Consequences: Fail-safe closure of critical production valves; total facility shutdown; flare event
ExistingSafeguards: Redundant air compressors; large capacity air receivers; cross-tie with nitrogen buffer
RecommendedActionsMitigations: Ensure N+1 redundancy for air compressors; conduct regular leak testing of the air distribution network
AppliesTo: Topside utility systems, control valves, pneumatic panels
